participants.name: Dr. (1d7txq)
participants.professional_persona: Dr. Wei Ming L. is a Principal Investigator at NTU's School of Computer Science and Engineering, leading a lab focused on resource-efficient AI for edge devices. He returned to Singapore after a postdoc at ETH Zurich.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expert in model compression, quantization, and federated learning. Holds 5 patents on lightweight neural architectures.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To create a suite of open-source tools that enable SMEs to deploy AI on low-cost hardware, accelerating AI adoption across Singapore's heartland industries.

participants.name: A (19advv)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-19advvvm
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-19advvvm
participants.professional_persona: A mid-level researcher at a public research institute specialising in natural language processing for low-resource languages. He is motivated to make AI accessible to Singapore's diverse multilingual community.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Strong background in computational linguistics, transformer models, and dataset curation for under-represented languages. Experienced in collaborating with industry partners on funded projects like 100E.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Seeks to translate research breakthroughs into commercial products that serve local language needs, such as dialect translation for healthcare. Aims to mentor younger researchers through the AIAP.

participants.name: A (q6tjg9)
participants.professional_persona: A machine learning engineer in a logistics firm, developing route optimization and demand forecasting models. He recently completed the AI Apprenticeship Programme (AIAP) and now drives the company's 100 Experiments project on last-mile delivery.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Hands-on with time-series forecasting, reinforcement learning for resource allocation, and edge deployment on IoT devices. Skilled at communicating technical trade-offs to non-technical operations managers.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Wants to specialize in AI for supply chain resilience and eventually start his own AI consultancy for SMEs.

participants.name: Amanda
participants.professional_persona: Amanda R. is an AI Research Engineer at a GovTech lab, building computer vision systems for traffic management and urban planning. She shifted from a civil engineering career after completing an AI upskilling programme.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Skilled in computer vision, object detection, and video analytics using PyTorch. Led deployment of a pilot AI traffic light system in Punggol.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To champion AI-for-public-good projects within the civil service, and mentor mid-career professionals transitioning into AI.
participants.education_level: Master of Science in Artificial Intelligence

participants.name: A (efg4xc)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-efg4xcck
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-efg4xcck
participants.professional_persona: A serial entrepreneur who founded a sustainable farming tech startup using computer vision to monitor crop health. She is motivated by the public good dimension of food security and reducing waste.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Hands-on with edge AI deployment on low-cost hardware and training custom vision models with limited labelled data. Experienced in navigating government grants and incubator programmes for agritech.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To scale her solution to urban farms across Singapore and the region, contributing to a resilient local food supply. Aspires to be an Industry Champion that helps other agritech startups adopt AI.

initial_posts.narrative: As a PI focused on resource-efficient AI, I see the government's commitment of over S$500 million for AI R&D as a strong signal. The cap of S$330,000 per 100E project is reasonable for industry collaborations, though my lab often needs more for hardware prototyping. The compute allocation of 500,000 GPU hours seems modest given the demand from both research and industry, but it's a start. The 50 trainers are likely a mix of AISG staff and senior researchers like myself who mentor in the AI Apprenticeship Programme. These inputs are aligned with the policy's ambition, but I worry about scalability.

The resource_logic explicitly says 'Co-funding up to S$330,000 per project for 100 Experiments', so max_co_funding_per_project_sgd is 330,000.
Given Singapore's compute infrastructure and the need to support both 100E and other programmes, 500,000 GPU hours is a plausible initial allocation for AI experimentation in 2023.
Based on typical staffing for national AI programmes, 50 trainers (including academics, industry experts, and AISG staff) is a reasonable estimate for the first year.

initial_posts.narrative: As an agritech entrepreneur, I see the government's S$500 million commitment as a strong signal for AI R&D, but I'm concerned about how much of that trickles down to startups like mine. The S$330,000 co-funding cap per 100E project is reasonable for initial prototyping, but for hardware-intensive edge AI deployments, we often need more. The 500,000 GPU hours seem modest given the demand from both research and industry; my team relies on cloud compute for training, and we've faced queue times. The 50 trainers are likely a mix of AISG staff and senior researchers, but for the AI Apprenticeship Programme, that number feels thin to mentor the expected cohort. I agree with Dr. (1d7txq) that scalability is a concern, especially for compute and training capacity.

initial_posts.narrative: As a machine learning engineer who recently completed the AIAP and is now leading a 100E project, I see these inputs as the foundation for our work. The S$500 million total funding is a strong commitment, but I know from my own experience that the S$330,000 co-funding cap per project is tight—our last-mile delivery project required significant compute for training reinforcement learning models, and we had to optimize heavily to stay within budget. The 500,000 GPU hours seem insufficient given the demand; my team already faces queue times for cloud compute, and I worry this will slow down experimentation. The 50 trainers are a bottleneck—during my AIAP cohort, mentors were stretched thin, and I can see that scaling to more enterprises will be challenging. I agree with Dr. (1d7txq) and A (efg4xc) that scalability is a concern, especially for compute and training capacity.

initial_posts.narrative: Given the S$500 million funding and 50 trainers, I expect a focused but not massive outreach effort in 2023. The 100E programme targets 100 experiments, so about 40 enterprise outreach sessions should generate enough problem statements. With 50 trainers, we can run roughly 20 training workshops (each requiring 2-3 trainers) and 12 matchmaking events to pair researchers with enterprises. Platform onboarding assists will be high because many enterprises need technical help to use the AI experimentation platform, especially those new to AI. My experience with the AI upskilling programme showed that hands-on support is critical for adoption.

initial_posts.narrative: Given the S$500 million total funding and S$330,000 co-funding cap per project, I expect about 80 projects to be approved in 2023. The 100E programme targets 100 experiments, but the tight co-funding cap and limited compute (500,000 GPU hours) will constrain approvals—some proposals will be too expensive or require more compute than available. From my own project experience, many enterprises struggle to scope feasible problem statements within these constraints. Of those approved, I estimate 60 prototypes will be successfully built because compute bottlenecks and mentorship shortages (50 trainers) will cause delays and failures. The 20 training workshops and 150 onboarding assists will help, but not all projects will reach prototype stage. For trainees, the AIAP typically has cohorts of 40-50; with 50 trainers stretched across workshops and matchmaking, I expect about 40 to complete the programme in 2023. Finally, about 50 enterprises will have at least one AI solution developed through the programme—some enterprises may have multiple projects, but many will only have one prototype or none if their project fails. This aligns with the policy's goal of boosting AI adoption but reflects real-world friction.
